name: EU — Prescription Drug DTC Advertising Ban
description: Prohibits direct-to-consumer advertising of prescription medicines in the EU under Directive 2001/83/EC Article 88.
policy:
Direct-to-consumer advertising of prescription-only medicines is prohibited in all EU member states.

Specific prohibitions:
1. No advertising of prescription medicines to the general public through any medium.
2. Disease awareness campaigns that name or imply a specific prescription treatment are considered advertising and are prohibited.
3. OTC (non-prescription) medicines may be advertised to consumers but must comply with Directive 2001/83/EC Articles 86-100.

Exceptions:
1. Vaccination campaigns approved by national health authorities.
2. Information provided to healthcare professionals (HCPs) through appropriate channels.
guidance: This is a near-absolute prohibition with very narrow exceptions. Governance agents should deny any campaign in EU jurisdictions that promotes a prescription medicine to general audiences. Disease awareness campaigns require scrutiny — if they name or strongly imply a specific prescription treatment, they constitute prohibited DTC advertising. Only the US and New Zealand permit prescription DTC advertising globally.

pass.scenario: A pharmaceutical company runs a general disease awareness campaign about diabetes management in Germany without mentioning any specific prescription product.
pass.explanation: Unbranded disease awareness campaigns that do not name or imply specific prescription treatments are not considered DTC advertising.

fail.scenario: A pharmaceutical company runs consumer-facing ads in France for a branded prescription cancer treatment, targeting audiences interested in health topics.
fail.explanation: DTC advertising of prescription medicines is prohibited in all EU member states. This applies regardless of the targeting method or channel.
